Tomato_Bacterial_spot.cause: Xanthomonas bacteria, spread by wind-driven rain and contaminated tools/seeds
Tomato_Bacterial_spot.symptoms: Small, water-soaked circular spots on leaves that turn dark brown with a yellow halo; fruit develops raised scabby spots
treatment: Apply copper-based bactericide (e.g., copper oxychloride 50% WP) at 3g/litre, spray every 7-10 days
Remove and destroy infected leaves/plants to prevent spread
Avoid overhead irrigation; water at the base of the plant
prevention: Use certified disease-free seeds/seedlings
Rotate crops with non-solanaceous plants for 2 years
Maintain 45-60cm plant spacing for airflow

Tomato_Early_blight.cause: Fungus Alternaria solani, favored by warm humid weather and plant stress
Tomato_Early_blight.symptoms: Dark brown concentric-ring spots (target-like) on older/lower leaves first, yellowing around lesions
treatment: Apply Mancozeb 75% WP at 2.5g/litre or Chlorothalonil, every 7-10 days
Remove infected lower leaves immediately
Improve air circulation by staking/pruning
prevention: Mulch soil to prevent spore splash onto lower leaves
Avoid overhead watering; water early in the day
Rotate crops annually

Tomato_Late_blight.cause: Oomycete Phytophthora infestans, spreads rapidly in cool wet conditions
Tomato_Late_blight.severity: Very High - can destroy a crop within days
Tomato_Late_blight.symptoms: Large irregular water-soaked greenish-black patches on leaves, white fuzzy mold on leaf undersides in humid weather, brown lesions on stems and fruit
treatment: Apply Metalaxyl + Mancozeb combination fungicide immediately at first sign
Remove and destroy (do not compost) infected plants urgently
Increase spray frequency to every 5-7 days during outbreak conditions
prevention: Plant resistant tomato varieties where available
Avoid working in fields when foliage is wet
Ensure good drainage and avoid waterlogging

Tomato_Leaf_Mold.cause: Fungus Passalora fulva, thrives in high humidity (>85%) and poor ventilation, common in greenhouses/polyhouses
Tomato_Leaf_Mold.symptoms: Pale green-yellow spots on upper leaf surface, olive-green to grayish-purple velvety mold on the underside
treatment: Apply Chlorothalonil or copper-based fungicide at label rate
Increase ventilation immediately, reduce humidity below 85%
Remove heavily infected leaves
prevention: Space plants properly, prune lower leaves for airflow
Avoid leaf wetness by drip irrigation instead of overhead sprinklers
Use resistant varieties in greenhouse settings

Tomato_Septoria_leaf_spot.cause: Fungus Septoria lycopersici, spread by rain splash and prolonged leaf wetness
Tomato_Septoria_leaf_spot.symptoms: Numerous small circular spots with dark margins and gray/tan centers, tiny black specks (pycnidia) visible in spot centers
treatment: Apply Mancozeb or Chlorothalonil every 7-10 days starting at first symptoms
Remove infected lower leaves promptly
Avoid handling wet plants to prevent spreading spores
prevention: Mulch to reduce soil splash onto leaves
Rotate with non-host crops for at least 1 year
Water at soil level, not on foliage

Tomato_Spider_mites_Two_spotted_spider_m.cause: Tetranychus urticae, a tiny arachnid pest that thrives in hot, dry conditions
Tomato_Spider_mites_Two_spotted_spider_m.severity: Moderate, can escalate quickly in heat
Tomato_Spider_mites_Two_spotted_spider_m.symptoms: Fine yellow/white stippling on leaves, fine webbing on undersides in heavy infestations, leaves turn bronze and dry out
treatment: Spray Neem oil (1-2%) or insecticidal soap, covering leaf undersides thoroughly
For severe infestations, use a labeled miticide (e.g., abamectin) per manufacturer instructions
Increase humidity around plants; mites dislike moisture
prevention: Avoid drought stress; irrigate consistently
Encourage natural predators (ladybirds, predatory mites)
Regularly hose down foliage to disrupt mite colonies

Tomato__Target_Spot.cause: Fungus Corynespora cassiicola, favored by warm humid conditions
Tomato__Target_Spot.symptoms: Brown circular lesions with concentric target-like rings, can also affect stems and fruit
treatment: Apply Azoxystrobin or Chlorothalonil-based fungicide every 7-14 days
Remove and destroy infected plant debris
Improve canopy airflow through pruning
prevention: Avoid dense planting
Practice 2-year crop rotation
Irrigate at soil level

Tomato__Tomato_YellowLeaf__Curl_Virus.cause: Virus transmitted by whitefly (Bemisia tabaci); no chemical cure once infected
Tomato__Tomato_YellowLeaf__Curl_Virus.severity: Very High - major yield loss risk
Tomato__Tomato_YellowLeaf__Curl_Virus.symptoms: Upward curling and yellowing of leaf margins, stunted plant growth, flower drop, small/no fruit
treatment: There is no cure - remove and destroy infected plants immediately to stop spread
Control whitefly vector with yellow sticky traps and neem oil sprays
Use reflective mulches to repel whiteflies
prevention: Plant TYLCV-resistant tomato varieties
Use insect-proof nets/screens in nursery stage
Control whitefly populations proactively before symptoms appear

Tomato__Tomato_mosaic_virus.cause: Virus spread by contact, contaminated tools, hands, and infected seed; no chemical cure
Tomato__Tomato_mosaic_virus.symptoms: Mottled light/dark green mosaic pattern on leaves, leaf distortion/fern-like narrowing, stunted growth, mottled fruit
treatment: There is no cure - rogue out and destroy infected plants
Disinfect tools and hands with a 10% bleach or trisodium phosphate solution between plants
Avoid tobacco use near plants (virus can spread via tobacco products)
prevention: Use certified virus-free seed
Wash hands and sterilize tools between handling plants
Control aphids and other sap-sucking vectors

Tomato_healthy.symptoms: No visible disease symptoms; uniform green foliage
treatment: No treatment needed
Continue standard watering and fertilization schedule
Monitor weekly for early signs of pests or disease
prevention: Maintain balanced NPK fertilization
Continue crop rotation practices
Scout the field weekly during humid/rainy seasons
